Provider_Developer_Analysis.header_bill:
This section provides a comparative analysis of the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ regarding their scope and related obligations, as found in the selected regulations. It should be noted that the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ analysed in this section, are the most general category of provider or developers of AI systems found across the different regulations. As such, this section does not include obligations for providers and developers of specific categories of AI systems or models, such as those of high-risk AI systems or frontier AI models, which are analysed in subsequent sections.
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of providers identified has been termed ‘providers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology, on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying it to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, article 50 specifies the kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).
Provider_Developer_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Regulatory trigger: reactive vs proactive approaches
The AIA adopts a proactive, ex-ante regulatory approach, whereby the placing on the market triggers obligations. In contrast, Texas HB 149 focuses on reactive, ex-post enforcement triggered by the Attorney General.
- Transparency requirements
The AIA puts in place transparency obligations for all providers of AI systems or GPAI models including the need of making persons aware that they are interacting with the AI system through system design, as well as using machine-readable marking of AI-generated content. A similar approach is adopted in Colorado SB 24-205, which requires all developers to disclose interaction with AI system to consumers. Whereas Texas HB 149, does not put in place transparency obligations for developers, it does so at the deployer level, albeit in a more limited manner, where governmental agencies and providers of healthcare services must disclose to the consumer the use of AI systems.
- Obligations
Texas’ HB 149 requires developers to provide certain documentation, including a description of the purpose, intended use, and deployment context of the AI system, the type of data used to train the system, limitations of the AI system and post-deployment monitoring and user safeguards. These requirements have some similarity with the AIA’s documentation requirements for providers of high-risk systems and GPAI models, such as descriptions on the type of data used to train the system, evaluation of system performance and post-deployment monitoring.
- Substantive filter: intention and framework compliance
In Texas, liability is largely contingent on proving a developer’s subjective "intent to discriminate". Conversely, in the AIA, penalties apply irrespective of the occurrence of harm and regardless the provider actually meant to cause harm. Moreover, in Texas, ‘substantial compliance’ with NIST Risk Management Framework exempts developers from liability. A similar approach is adopted in Colorado’s SB 24-205.
- Compliance grace period: the right to cure
Both Colorado and Texas provides a 60-day opportunity to cure a violation. Such a rebuttal or cure period is not in place in the AIA.

Provider_Developer_Analysis.sub_table: Provider of high-risk AI systems / developer of high-risk AI systems
Provider_Developer_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Scope of high-risk
In the AIA a system is high-risk if it 1) acts as a safety component of a product, or is a product itself, covered by specific Union harmonisation legislation (e.g., machinery, toys, medical devices), and 2) is listed in any of the following areas: biometrics; critical infrastructure; educational and vocational training; employment, workers' management and access to self-employment; essential private and public services and benefits; law enforcement; migration, asylum and border control management; administration of justice and democratic processes. like law enforcement, migration, or critical infrastructure (AIA, Article 6 and Annex III). This is linked to a broad understanding of risk as the probability of occurrence and severity of harm (AIA, Article 3).
In Colorado’s SB 24-205, a system is deemed high-risk if it is a "substantial factor" in “consequential decisions” made in the following areas: education enrolment or an education opportunity; employment or an employment opportunity; a financial or lending service; an essential government service; health-care services; housing; insurance; or a legal service (§6-1-1701). This is linked to an understanding of risk limited to “algorithmic discrimination” that is, when the use of the AI system leads to “an unlawful differential treatment or impact that disfavors an individual or group of individuals on the basis of their actual or perceived age, color, disability, ethnicity, genetic information, limited proficiency in the English language, national origin, race,  religion, reproductive health, sex, veteran status” (§6-1-1701).
- High-risk areas
Both regulations share a common understanding of some of the contexts or areas wherein the AI system’s development and deployment is considered high-risk, namely in education, employment and “essential public services and benefits” (AIA, Annex III (5)) or “essential government services” (Colorado SB 24-205). 
However, important differences exist between the two regulatory texts. While Colorado’s SB 24-205 excludes “access and enjoyment of essential private services”, as listed in the AIA, it lists healthcare-services, housing, insurance, and financial and legal services as part of high-risk contexts. Notable exclusions from Colorado’s SB 24-205, when compared with the EU AIA, include biometrics, critical infrastructure, migration, asylum and border control management.
- Substantial modification
In both regulations, providers or developers of high-risk AI systems include those who ‘substantially modify’ (AIA) or ‘intentionally and substantially modify’ a high-risk AI system. Note the layer of intentionality in Colorado’s SB 24-205, which means that, a developer is not liable for the high-risk AI system if the substantial modification was not deliberate, even if changes made to the system might result  in “any new reasonably foreseeable risk of algorithmic discrimination” (§6-1-1701).
- Obligations
Where the AIA puts in a place a more granular set of obligations and documentation for providers of high-risk AI systems, Colorado’s SB 24-205 primarily addresses transparency and prevention of algorithmic discrimination. 
Both regulations prioritise risk management but, while the AIA requires putting in place a quality management system (Article 16) and a post-market monitoring system (Article 72), Colorado’s SB 24-205 obligations for developers are centred on public disclosure, since core risk management requirements fall under the remit of deployers who “shall implement a risk management policy and program” (§6-1-1703). 
This means that the AIA more promptly addresses the entire lifecycle of the AI system and places an emphasis on the systematic review and updating of the quality management system, including putting an accountability framework in place and strategies for regulatory compliance, a risk management system for identifying and mitigating risks, having a heavy focus of technical documentation. Colorado’s SB 24-205 is primarily focused on disclosure of information, requiring developers to state the types of AI systems developed and how risks of algorithmic discrimination are managed. 
- Registration
The AIA mandates providers to register the AI system and its providers in an EU database, provide a declaration of conformity, as well as affix the CE marking to the high-risk system prior to placing it on the market. Conformity assessment must be completed through internal self-assessment, with the exception of AI systems deployed in the area of biometrics, limited critical infrastructure and AI systems which are safety components of products, which must undergo third-party conformity assessment (AIA, Article 6, Article 43, Annex III). 
No such provisions exist in Colorado’s SB 24-205, where obligations are limited to a developer’s public website statement summarising its high-risk AI systems.
- Rebuttal and standards
The AIA allows a provider to argue that their system should not be classified as high-risk in the first place, but once it is classified as such, the compliance rules are rigid (Article 6).
While Colorado’s SB 24-205 does not have this kind of rebuttal option, it puts in place an opportunity to cure violations. This means that developers can fix their AI systems and avoid penalties. Importantly, should a developer prove that they followed recognised standards like NIST AI RMF or ISO 42001, they can legally rebut the accusation of a violation.

Provider_Developer_Analysis.text: This section provides a comparative analysis of the terms ‘Provider of general-purpose AI models’ ‘covered provider’ and ‘developer’ regarding their scope and obligations, as stated in the selected regulations, namely in the EU AIA and California’s SB942 and AB2013. Despite differences in the scope of the terms, with the EU AIA addressing GPAI models and California’s SB942 and AB2013 addressing generative AI systems, these terms were deemed sufficiently comparable to be analysed in tandem. The three regulatory texts can be said to have a overlapping understanding of ‘provider of general-purpose AI models’  (AIA), ‘covered provider’ (SB942) and ‘developer’ (AB2013), due to the GPAI models addressed in the EU AIA including generative AI models (AI Act, Recital 99).

Provider_Developer_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Scope and thresholds
The AIA defines GPAI models based on their generality, whereas California’s regulations apply based functionality – that is, generation of synthetic content – for AB 2013, and reach, namely having over 1M monthly users/visitors for SB 942. 
- Obligations: transparency as a common requirement
All three regulatory frameworks, that is, the EU AIA and California’s AB 2013 and SB 942, put transparency obligations in place. In the AIA, transparency requirements are centred on documentation on the model and training datasets, with a similar approach being adopted in California’s AB 2013, where developers are required to publish a high-level public statement detailing data provenance, characteristics and copyright.
California’s SB 942 targets transparency understood as output transparency as AI-generated content for consumers (manifest disclosure) and through latent disclosure. It should be noted that, whereas the AIA does not mandate similar requirements for providers of GPAI models, it does so at the level of providers GPAI systems (AIA, Article 50), that is, an AI system based on a general-purpose AI model (AIA, Article 3), and through a commitment for provider of GPAI models of systemic risk who are signatories of the CoP (measure 10.2).
-Obligations: governance
A core difference across the regulatory frameworks is the requirement for comprehensive governance characterising the AIA, which covers providers of GPAI models, including those with high-impact capabilities (i.e., GPAI models with systemic risk). In California, more comprehensive governance obligations are limited to frontier systems with high-impact capabilities, namely those using over > 10²⁶ FLOPs in compute.

Provider_Developer_Analysis.header_bill:
This section provides a comparative analysis of the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ regarding their scope and related obligations, as found in the selected regulations. It should be noted that the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ analysed in this section, are the most general category of provider or developers of AI systems found across the different regulations. As such, this section does not include obligations for providers and developers of specific categories of AI systems or models, such as those of high-risk AI systems or frontier AI models, which are analysed in subsequent sections.
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of providers identified has been termed ‘providers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology, on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying it to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, article 50 specifies the kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).

Provider_Developer_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Scope
The AIA takes a qualitative approach based on high-impact capabilities to determine obligations for providers of GPAI models with systemic risk (GPAISR), stating the compute threshold of 10²⁵ FLOPs as an indicative criterion. While the AIA’s does not limit the scope of GPAI models with systemic risk to compute thresholds, the current indicative criterion is lower than that of California’s SB 53 and New York’s S8828, which designate frontier models with catastrophic risks as those starting at 10²⁶ FLOPs in compute. 
In the U.S., there are no significant regulatory requirements for developers of frontier models below the 10²⁶ FLOPs threshold. In contrast, in the EU, since the FLOPs threshold is only indicative, obligations can apply to models designated by the European Commission as GPAISR irrespective of training compute (Article 52(1)). However, the flexibility provided by the AIA qualitative approach also operates in reverse, with providers of GPAISR having the possibility to demonstrate that their model should not be classified as a GPAISR for not presenting systemic risks despite its high-impact capabilities.  Such provisions are not existent in California’s and New York’s regulatory frameworks. 
It should be noted that both the AIA (Article 51(3)) and California’s SB 53 (22757.14(a)) put in place provisions for the revision of these thresholds, which might be updated to respond to the developments in AI capabilities. 
- Tiered regulatory requirements
The AIA puts in place equal obligations for all providers of GPAI models with systemic risk, but acknowledges that simplified ways of compliance must be provided to SMEs and SMCs, including startups – for example, allowing for less detailed model reports (CoP, 7) –  to ensure measures are proportionate with they size and capacity (CoP, recital (h)).
California (SB 53) and New York (S8828) have different requirements for smaller and larger frontier developers, putting forward a tiered approach where ‘frontier developers’ have a limited set of obligations and ‘large frontier developers’ – that is, those who have over $500M in annual gross revenues – must comply with a larger set of regulatory requirements. A similar approach was adopted in New York’s A6453B, with only those spending over $1M in compute costs being covered by the regulation.
- Obligations: risk management
Obligations for provider of GPAI models with systemic risk are comparable to those in place in California and New York for large frontier developers. All regulations require these providers and developers to perform continuous post-market monitoring of their models and put in place risk management frameworks that should be reviewed, at least, once per year: the AIA’s ‘Safety and Security Framework’ and California’s and New York’s ‘Frontier AI Framework’. 
While the California’s SB 53 (22757.12.) and New York’s S8828 (1421) specifically require the disclosure of the approach adopted to using third parties “to assess the potential for catastrophic risks and the effectiveness of mitigations of catastrophic risks”, the EU’s CoP presents the involvement of third-party evaluators as a suggestion in measure 7.4.
Despite some nuances across these frameworks, they all emphasise the relevance of determining acceptable risk and put in place important cybersecurity measures. It should be noted that, in the two US states, such obligations do not apply to frontier developers with less than $500M in annual gross revenues.
Overall, with regards to risk management, the AIA supplemented by the CoP, is more detailed and prescriptive. while under SB53 developers must create, implement, and publish a ‘Frontier AI Framework’ and are assessed based on whether they comply with it. 
- Obligations: copyright
One core difference between the EU and the US, is the AIA’s requirement for putting in place a copyright compliance policy that provides a mechanism for creators to opt-out of training (Article 53; CoP, 1). In the US, developers must provide a summary of datasets (including copyrighted ones), but there is no such opt-out mechanism in place. Specific AI copyright bills (e.g., California AB-412) have failed to pass so far.
- Notification and registration
As per the AIA, providers must notify the European Commission when their GPAI models meet (or are expect to meet) the systemic risk threshold (Article 52).
A similar approach is adopted in New York’s S8828, with large developers being obliged to file a disclosure statement, to be updated every two years, and make a pro-rata payment contributing towards the regulatory costs of state administration (1428).  California’s SB53 does not have such requirements in place. 
- Reporting: incidents and risks
While incident reporting is mandatory in the AIA, SB53 and S8828, with the US states having much shorter deadlines for doing so (24 to 72 hours versus 25 hours to 15 days in the EU), the US-states put in place additional reporting obligations. California and New York require quarterly reports summarising catastrophic risk assessments. 
The AIA is less strict with periodic reporting, requiring providers of GPAISR to provide an updated model report to the AI Office at least every six months only in the case of changes in the model’s capabilities, propensities or affordances (CoP, 7.6). As such, models that are considered substantially similar or similarly safe or safer (as per CoP, Appendix 2.2) are exempt from periodic reporting, unless significant changes are made to the GPAISR.
- Exemptions
While open source AI models are exempt from certain obligations under the AIA (Article 53), this is not the case for GPAI models with systemic risks. In New York, accredited colleges and universities, as well as members of the Empire AI consortium (universities partnership), are exempt from all obligations under S8828 and A6453B.

Provider_Developer_Analysis.header_bill:
This section provides a comparative analysis of the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ regarding their scope and related obligations, as found in the selected regulations. It should be noted that the terms ‘provider’ and ‘developer’ analysed in this section, are the most general category of provider or developers of AI systems found across the different regulations. As such, this section does not include obligations for providers and developers of specific categories of AI systems or models, such as those of high-risk AI systems or frontier AI models, which are analysed in subsequent sections.
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of providers identified has been termed ‘providers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology, on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying it to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, article 50 specifies the kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).

Deployer

Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.header_bill:
This section compares the terms and obligations for deployers of limited-risk AI systems put in place in the AIA, and for deployers of AI systems put in place in Colorado's SB24-205 and Texas' HB 149.  This is the most general category of deployers of AI systems found across the different regulations, since the AIA puts in place additional obligations for deployers of high-risk AI systems and GPAI systems, and the same occurs for deployers of high-risk AI systems in Colorado’s SB 24-205. Texas’ HB 149 only places obligations for deployers at this higher level of generality, that is, without specifying contexts of deployment nor the differentiated technical nature of specific types of AI systems, as is the case for GPAI systems. 
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of deployers identified has been termed ‘deployers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, Article 50 identifies specific kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).
Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Regulatory trigger: reactive vs proactive approaches
The AIA adopts a proactive, ex-ante regulatory approach, whereby the deployment of the system triggers obligation. A similar approach is adopted in Colorado's SB 25-205, which mandates the disclosure of AI interaction upon AI system deployment. In contrast, Texas' HB 149 focuses on reactive, ex-post enforcement triggered by the Attorney General, with the exception of governmental agencies and providers of healthcare services, which must comply with transparency obligations upon system deployment.
- Additional obligations for public agencies
Whereas the AIA’s and Colorado’s obligations apply to deployers irrespective of them being public agencies or institutions, in Texas' HB 149 an additional layer of obligations is in place for governmental agencies and providers of healthcare services. Whereas all deployers have the general disclosure obligations post-deployment and upon request by the Attorney General, additional obligations centred on transparency apply for governmental agencies and healthcare services apply upon AI system deployment. 
- Transparency requirements
The AIA puts in place transparency obligations for deployers of limited-risk AI systems including the need of making persons aware that they are interacting with the AI system through system design, as well as using machine-readable marking of AI-generated content (Article 50).
A similar approach is adopted in Colorado’s SB 24-205, but transparency requirements are limited to disclose of AI-interaction to consumers. In Texas' HB 149, transparency obligations applicable at the deployer level remain limited to governmental agencies and providers of healthcare services, who are required to disclose the use of AI systems to the consumer. Concretely, healthcare services must disclose when an  AI system is being used in the provision of a service or treatment. 
- Obligations: general information disclosure
Texas’ HB 149 requires all deployers to provide certain documentation upon request by public authorities, including a description of the purpose, intended use, and deployment context of the AI system, the type of data used to train the system, limitations of the AI system and post-deployment monitoring and user safeguards. These requirements have some similarity with the EU AIA’s documentation requirements for deployers of high-risk systems, namely post-deployment monitoring of the system and transparency requirements.
- Substantive filter: intention and framework compliance
In Texas’ HB 149, liability is largely contingent on proving a provider’s "intent to discriminate". Conversely, in the AIA – which is not a liability framework – penalties apply regardless of whether 1) harm has occurred and 2) the provider actually meant to cause harm.
Moreover, in Texas’s HB 149 and Colorado’s SB 24-205, compliance with NIST Risk Management Framework exempts providers from liability.
- Compliance grace period: the right to cure
Both U.S. states, Texas and Colorado, provide an opportunity to cure violations. Such a rebuttal or cure period is not in place in the AIA.

Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.header_bill:
This section compares the terms and obligations for deployers of limited-risk AI systems put in place in the AIA, and for deployers of AI systems put in place in Colorado's SB24-205 and Texas' HB 149.  This is the most general category of deployers of AI systems found across the different regulations, since the AIA puts in place additional obligations for deployers of high-risk AI systems and GPAI systems, and the same occurs for deployers of high-risk AI systems in Colorado’s SB 24-205. Texas’ HB 149 only places obligations for deployers at this higher level of generality, that is, without specifying contexts of deployment nor the differentiated technical nature of specific types of AI systems, as is the case for GPAI systems. 
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of deployers identified has been termed ‘deployers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, Article 50 identifies specific kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).
Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.sub_table: Deployer / supplier of high-risk AI systems

While Utah’s SB 226 applies to supplier of generative AI systems, it distinguishes between the type of interactions prompted by the generative AI system: generative AI systems deployed in the context of regulated occupations are considered to operate in high-risk domains. For this reason, the term has been included for comparative analysis in this section – in addition to the section DEPLOYER OF GPAI SYSTEMS / SUPPLIER OF GENERATIVE AI SYSTEMS – because certain provisions target only high-risk domains.

Texas’ HB 149 only places obligations for deployers at this higher level of generality, that is, without specifying contexts of deployment nor the differentiated technical nature of specific types of AI systems, as is the case for GPAI systems. 
With regards to the AIA, the most general category of deployers identified has been termed ‘deployers of limited-risk AI systems’. Whereas the term “limited-risk” is not defined as a legal category in the AIA, it is a classificatory label used by the European Commission of which operative scope lies in Article 50. The Commission mobilises the “limited-risk” terminology on its AIA-dedicated webpage, specifically tying to Article 50 (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai).  Secondly, Recital 132 provides a substantive framing for this category of providers, stating that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not” (AIA, Recital 132). Thirdly, as outlined in the table below, Article 50 identifies specific kinds of AI systems that fit in this category, namely: AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons (50(1)), generating synthetic image, audio, video or text content (50(2)), and deep-fake-generating systems (50(4)), plus AI-generated text published to inform the public on matters of public interest (50(5)).
Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.sub_table: Deployer / supplier of high-risk AI systems
Deployer_Supplier_Analysis.text:
Interpretative notes
- Scope of high-risk
In the AIA a system is high-risk if it is a safety component of a regulated product or if it is intended to operate in specific areas like law enforcement, migration, or critical infrastructure. This is linked to a broad understanding of risk as the probability and severity of harm. 
In Colorado’s SB 24-205, the fact that its understanding of risk is limited to “algorithmic discrimination” means that a system is deemed high-risk if it is a "substantial factor" in “consequential decisions” taken in listed circumstances, namely: education enrolment or an education opportunity; employment or an employment opportunity; a financial or lending service; an essential government service; health-care services; housing; insurance; or a legal service.
In Utah's SB 226, high-risk systems are generative AI systems deployed in regulated occupations, which include, for example, physicians, surgeons, accountants, psychologist, mental health chatbots, etc. 
- High-risk areas
Both the AIA and Colorado's SB24-205 share a common understanding of some of the contexts or areas wherein the AI system’s development and deployment is considered high-risk, namely in education, employment and “essential public services and benefits” (AI Act, Annex III (5)) or “essential government services” (Colorado SB24-205). While Colorado’s SB24-205 excludes a specific mention of “essential private services”, as listed in the AIA, it lists healthcare-services, housing, insurance, and financial and legal services as part of high-risk contexts. Notable exclusions from Colorado’s SB24-205, when compared with the AIA, include biometrics, critical infrastructure, migration, asylum and border control management.
In Utah’s SB226, high-risk relates to services provided in the context of regulated occupations, which require state license or certification. These occupations cover sectors such as healthcare (physicians, nurses, dentists), construction/trades (contractors, plumbers), mortgage providers (banker), etc.
- Transparency as common requirement
Transparency is the only common obligation for deployers of high-risk AI systems across all three regulations. In the AIA, this implies informing individuals that AI is being used as well as disclosing AI-generated content as artificially generated or manipulated. In Colorado's SB24-205, transparency requirements are centred on the need to disclose that AI has been deployed in making a consequential decision. In Utah’s SB226, transparency means disclosing interaction with a generative AI system in services provided in the context of regulated occupations. 
- Obligations
The AIA and Colorado's SB24-205 require formal impact assessments to be conducted prior to deploying high-risk AI systems. The EU's impact assessment obligations focus on fundamental rights and data protection impacts. Colorado's, in turn, targets the analysis and mitigation of risks related to algorithmic discrimination, having some similarities with the AIA's conformity assessment requirements for providers of high-risk AI systems. 
While Colorado's SB24-205, mentions oversight measures as part of the safeguards to be described upon conducting an impact assessment of high-risk AI systems, the AIA is more explicit, having a dedicated article requiring deployers to put in place natural persons overseeing high-risk AI systems.
AI literacy and deployers' registration are unique obligations of the AIA.
- Right to explanation and appeal
Both the AIA and Colorado's SB24-205 put in place obligations for deployers to explain the role of the AI system in the decision-making process in case of adverse decisions. Colorado's SB24-205 goes one step further in requiring deployers to provide consumers with the opportunity to appeal the decision and allow for human review. 
- Exemptions and compliance grace period
In Colorado, deployers of high-risk AI systems who employ less than 50 full-time equivalent employees, are exempt from the obligations stated in SB24-205. Moreover, compliance with NIST Risk Management Framework exempts providers from liability. If a deployer commits a violation of the bill, it has the opportunity to cure it.

High-risk AI system_ANALYSIS.header_bill: This section compares the definition and scope of high-risk AI system and high-risk AI interaction across the AIA, Colorado’ SB 24-205 and Utah’s SB 226.
High-risk AI system_ANALYSIS.text:
Interpretative notes
- Definition
All three regulations under analysis adopt a different approach to defining high-risk AI systems or high-risk AI interactions. The AIA adopts a domain-based approach, which means that AI systems intended to operate in specific domains, such as employment, healthcare or education, are classified as high-risk.
Colorado adopts a similar approach when it comes to the domains identified, but what triggers belonging to this category of AI systems is the fact that the AI system makes, or is a substantial factor in making, a decision in these contexts.
In Utah, high-risk is based on the nature of the interaction, which SB 226 targets only in relation to interactions with generative AI systems.
- Exemptions
Both the AIA and Colorado consider AI systems that perform narrow procedural tasks, improve the result of a previously completed human activity or detect decision-making patterns exempt from the category of high-risk systems. No such exclusions are in place in Utah.

Risk_ANALYSIS.header_bill: This section compares the definition and scope of systemic and catastrophic risk across the AIA, California’s SB 53 and New York’s S8828.
Risk_ANALYSIS.text:
Interpretative notes
- Definition
Under the AIA’s systemic risk is tied to the high-impact capabilities of GPAI models. While the 10²⁵ FLOPs is an indicative criterion for general-purpose AI models with systemic risk, these capabilities should be “evaluated on the basis of appropriate technical tools and methodologies, including indicators and benchmarks”, to be facilitated by the European Commission through delegated acts (Article 51). 
In contrast, the approach to catastrophic risk in California and New York is only compute-based, applying to models trained using > 10²⁶ FLOPs in compute.
- Scope
California and New York restrict the scope of catastrophic risks to an exhaustive list of three activities, including, assistance in creation or release of CBRN weapons, loss of control and murder.
In the AIA, the applicability of systemic risk is open-ended and broad. The CoP provides further detail on the understanding of systemic risk under the AIA, including on the type, nature, sources and specific systemic risks (Appendix 1). The different types of systemic risks, already captured in Article 3, are risks to public health, safety, public security, fundamental rights and to society as a whole. The nature of systemic risks ranges from three essential characteristics, namely high-impact capabilities, impact on the Union market and propagation at scale across the value chain already outlined in Article 3, to six contributing characteristics, including capability- and reach-dependent, high velocity, compounding or cascading risk reactions, risks that are difficult or impossible to reverse, and risks that have asymmetric impact. The CoP further specifies model capabilities, properties and affordances that can be a source of systemic risk. Lastly, specific systemic risks are outlined, namely, CBRN-related risks and loss of control – aligned with California’s SB 53 and New York’s S8828 scope for catastrophic risk – as well as cyber offence and harmful manipulation.
- Systemic vs catastrophic risks
One key difference between the EU and the U.S., is the approach to risk. In the AIA, systemic risk is defined qualitatively, being inherently more adaptive and open to interpretation. It pertains to significant impacts, negative effects and reach.
In contrast, in California’s SB 53 and New York’s S8828, catastrophic risk is defined quantitatively, through number of deaths and serious injuries, as well as damage costs.
- Exemptions
While the AIA does not put any exemptions in place, California’s SB 53 and New York’s S8828 consider that whenever information disclosed in model outputs is already publicly available in a similar form, is part of lawful government activity and when the harm is caused through combining the frontier model with other software, it does not amount to catastrophic risk.
